final.content:
The user wants a complete final research report based on the evidence already gathered. I need to synthesize all the information from the web searches and fetches into a structured report covering:

1. GPU rendering performance (AutoCAD, Revit, Lumion)
2. Thermal management under sustained CAD loads in warm environments
3. RAM expandability toward 128GB
4. Enterprise support options in UAE
5. 5-year TCO factors including warranties and battery lifecycle

I need to compare Dell Precision 5690, HP ZBook Fury 16 G11, and Lenovo ThinkPad P1 Gen 7.

Let me organize the evidence:

**Dell Precision 5690:**
- CPU: Intel Core Ultra 5/7/9 (Meteor Lake-H), up to Core Ultra 9 185H
- GPU: Up to RTX 5000 Ada (16GB), also RTX 4000 Ada, RTX 3500 Ada, RTX 2000 Ada (8GB, 95W), RTX 1000 Ada
- RAM: Soldered LPDDR5x, up to 64GB only (16GB, 32GB, 64GB configs). NOT upgradeable after purchase. Cannot reach 128GB.
- Weight: ~2.17-2.31 kg
- Battery: 98 Wh
- Price in UAE: Base config ~AED 8,490; RTX 2000 Ada/32GB config ~AED 13,513-13,860
- Thermals: CPU hits 91°C under medium stress (concerning), sustains ~58W after 15min at 72°C. GPU RTX 2000 Ada sustains ~89W at 76°C after 30min. Has vapor chamber on high-end configs, conventional cooling on lower. Gets warm under load.
- GPU TDP: RTX 5000 Ada up to 175W, RTX 2000 Ada around 95W in the 5690.

**HP ZBook Fury 16 G11:**
- CPU: Intel Core i9-14900HX (Raptor Lake-HX Refresh) or i5/i7 HX, desktop-class
- GPU: Up to RTX 5000 Ada (16GB), RTX 4000 Ada, RTX 3500 Ada (12GB, tested at 105W TDP)
- RAM: 4 SODIMM slots, supports up to 128GB DDR5-5600 ECC or non-ECC. User upgradeable.
- Weight: ~2.55 kg, thicker (28.5mm)
- Battery: 95 Wh
- Price in UAE: RTX 3500/32GB ~AED 17,140-17,580; RTX Pro 2000/32GB ~AED 13,850
- Thermals: HP specs say system performance may reduce above 32°C (89.6°F). Under heavy load bottom gets "almost scalding hot", fans blast. Doesn't throttle badly according to some reviews. Idle temps of 70°C+ reported by some users (possibly poor paste). Operating temp 0-35°C.
- MIL-STD 810H certified.

**Lenovo ThinkPad P1 Gen 7:**
- CPU: Intel Core Ultra 5/7/9 (Meteor Lake-H), up to Core Ultra 9 185H
- GPU: Up to RTX 3000 Ada (8GB), RTX 2000 Ada, RTX 1000 Ada (6GB), OR consumer GeForce RTX 4060/4070. No RTX 4000/5000 Ada options. NotebookCheck says "less GPU performance than predecessor" and "high-end GPU options no longer available."
- RAM: LPCAMM2 module, world's first. Up to 64GB max (1x 64GB LPCAMM2). User replaceable/upgradeable but single module limits to 64GB currently. Cannot reach 128GB.
- Weight: Thinner/lighter design (~1.9 kg based on P1 G6 comparison)
- Battery: 90 Wh
- Price in UAE: Core Ultra 9/64GB/RTX 2000 Ada ~AED 13,289
- Thermals: Operating temp 5°C to 35°C. CPU can hit 103°C, GPU 88°C under "Best Performance" mode. Reports of running hot under prolonged workloads. Strange behavior where battery mode is cooler/quieter than AC.
- LPCAMM2 saves 64% space vs SO-DIMM, 61% less active power.

**GPU Rendering / Lumion:**
- Lumion relies primarily on GPU PassMark score. Ray tracing requires RTX support.
- RTX 5000 Ada > RTX 4000 Ada > RTX 3500 Ada > RTX 3000 Ada > RTX 2000 Ada
- RTX 5000 Ada: 9728 CUDA cores, 16GB VRAM, up to 175W, 42.6 TFLOPS
- RTX 4000 Ada: 7424 CUDA cores, 12GB VRAM, up to 175W
- RTX 3500 Ada: 5120 CUDA cores, 12GB VRAM, tested at 105W in ZBook
- RTX 3000 Ada: 4608 CUDA cores, 8GB VRAM
- RTX 2000 Ada: 3072 CUDA cores, 8GB VRAM
- Lumion 4K rendering needs minimum 8GB VRAM.
- HP ZBook Fury can be configured with highest-end GPUs (up to RTX 5000 Ada). Dell 5690 also up to RTX 5000 Ada. Lenovo P1 Gen 7 maxes out at RTX 3000 Ada or RTX 4070 consumer.
- For CAD (AutoCAD, Revit): These are often CPU-single-thread bound for 3D graphics, but Revit rendering and Lumion are GPU-heavy. ISV certifications matter.
- Dell and HP both emphasize ISV certifications for AutoCAD, Revit, SolidWorks.

**Thermal Management in Warm Environments (Dubai):**
- Dubai office ambient temps often 25-30°C+, potentially higher in summer if AC issues.
- HP ZBook Fury 16 G11: HP's own specs state "System performance may be reduced above 32°C (89.6°F)" [lap4worx quickspecs]. This is critical for Dubai.
- Dell Precision 5690: CPU reaches 91°C in medium stress tests (LaptopMedia), 72°C after sustained throttling to 58W. GPU sustains 76°C at 89W.
- Lenovo P1 Gen 7: CPU up to 103°C under load (NotebookCheck), GPU 88°C. Runs hot under prolonged workloads.
- The HP Fury is thicker (28.5mm) with more thermal mass/fans but still reports "scalding hot" bottom. Dell 5690 uses vapor chamber on high-end configs but test unit had conventional. Lenovo is thinner with less cooling headroom.
- For warm environments, thicker chassis (HP) may handle sustained loads better despite external temps, but HP explicitly warns about >32°C performance reduction.

**RAM Expandability to 128GB:**
- HP ZBook Fury 16 G11: 4 SODIMM slots, supports up to 128GB (4x32GB) ECC or non-ECC DDR5-5600. Confirmed by HP specs [support.hp.com, quickspecs].
- Dell Precision 5690: Soldered LPDDR5x, max 64GB. Not upgradeable after purchase. [Dell owner's manual, technical guidebook].
- Lenovo ThinkPad P1 Gen 7: Single LPCAMM2 slot, max 64GB. Upgradeable but current modules limited to 64GB. [PSREF spec sheet, Tom's Hardware].
- Clear winner: HP ZBook Fury. Dell and Lenovo cannot reach 128GB.

**Enterprise Support in UAE:**
- Dell: ProSupport Suite available in UAE. ProSupport Plus offers 24x7, NBD onsite, accidental damage, hard drive retention, extended battery service. UAE phone support: 8000 441 3889 (business hours), 8000 441 3745 (24x7 English). [dell.com/support/contents/en-ae]
- Lenovo: Premier Support and Premier Support Plus available in UAE. 24/7/365, local language support, NBD onsite in select markets, single point of contact. [lenovo.com/ae/en/premier-support-plus/]
- HP: HP Care Pack available. NBD onsite options. 1-year standard on some UAE listings, upgradable to 3-year. Local UAE resellers like Al Ershad, LastBestPrice offer configurations with 3-year warranty.
- All three have local UAE presence and business support channels. Dell and Lenovo publish UAE-specific support pages. HP uses partner/reseller channels heavily.

**5-Year TCO Factors:**
- Purchase price (UAE):
  - Dell Precision 5690 (Core Ultra 7, 32GB, RTX 2000 Ada, 3yr): ~AED 13,513-13,860
  - HP ZBook Fury 16 G11 (i9-14900HX, 32GB, RTX 3500 Ada, 3yr): ~AED 17,140
  - Lenovo ThinkPad P1 Gen 7 (Core Ultra 9, 64GB, RTX 2000 Ada, 3yr): ~AED 13,289
  - (Note: configs vary; Lenovo seems competitively priced with 64GB)
- Warranties: Standard 1-3 years. For 5-year TCO, extending warranty to 3-5 years is critical. Dell and HP offer up to 5-year Care Packs / ProSupport.
- Battery lifecycle: Dell data suggests ~300 charge cycles normal life; starting year 2, ~50% of batteries need replacement each year [Dell whitepaper]. HP batteries align with device warranty or 1000 charge cycles (whichever comes first) since 2017. For 5-year use, expect at least 1 battery replacement, possibly 2.
- Battery replacement costs: Often not covered under standard warranty after year 1 or 3. Dell Extended Battery Service extends up to year 4. HP offers battery-only Care Pack.
- RAM limitations: Dell's soldered 64GB max means if workload grows beyond 64GB, machine must be replaced rather than upgraded. HP's 128GB capability extends useful life.
- Thermal stress in warm climates: Higher temps reduce component longevity (battery, capacitors, solder joints). Machines that run cooler/throttle less will last longer.
- Resale value: HP Fury and Dell Precision tend to hold value better than Lenovo P-series in workstation market due to expandability/ISV certs.

**Quantitative Work:**
- RAM capacity: HP 128GB vs Dell 64GB vs Lenovo 64GB
- GPU tiers: Dell max RTX 5000 Ada, HP max RTX 5000 Ada, Lenovo max RTX 3000 Ada
- Thermal limits: HP >32°C warning; Dell 91°C CPU stress; Lenovo 103°C CPU stress
- Prices: AED ~13,500 Dell (32GB), AED ~17,100 HP (32GB RTX3500), AED ~13,300 Lenovo (64GB)
- Battery: Dell 98Wh, HP 95Wh, Lenovo 90Wh
- Weight: Dell ~2.2kg, HP ~2.6kg, Lenovo ~1.9kg

I should structure the report with clear headings and cite sources inline.

Let me double-check if I have any conflicting info. The Dell Precision 5690 memory - the search results clearly say soldered, not upgradeable, max 64GB. HP ZBook Fury has 4 slots, 128GB. Lenovo P1 Gen 7 has LPCAMM2, max 64GB per specs.

For GPU options, NotebookCheck says Lenovo P1 Gen 7 "high-end GPU options of the predecessor are no longer available" and max is RTX 3000 Ada or RTX 4070. Dell and HP both offer up to RTX 5000 Ada.

Thermal management: HP's official quickspec says "System performance may be reduced above 32°C (89.6°F)". This is a major factor for Dubai. Dell Precision 5690 with conventional cooling hits 91°C on CPU in medium stress. Lenovo hits 103°C CPU.

Support: All have UAE support. Dell has ProSupport Plus with NBD onsite. Lenovo has Premier Support Plus with NBD in select markets. HP has Care Packs.

Now write the report.**Workstation Laptop Comparison for an 8-Designer Architectural Firm in Dubai**
*Dell Precision 5690 vs. HP ZBook Fury 16 G11 vs. Lenovo ThinkPad P1 Gen 7*

---

### 1. GPU Rendering Performance for AutoCAD, Revit & Lumion

All three machines target professional visualization, but their GPU ceilings differ significantly.

**Maximum GPU Tier Available**
- **Dell Precision 5690:** Up to NVIDIA RTX 5000 Ada (16 GB GDDR6, 95–175 W TGP, 9,728 CUDA cores) [https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/products/workstations/professional-laptops/compare/]
- **HP ZBook Fury 16 G11:** Up to NVIDIA RTX 5000 Ada (16 GB GDDR6) [https://www.notebookcheck.net/Nvidia-RTX-3500-Ada-performance-debut-HP-ZBook-Fury-16-G11-workstation-review.870630.0.html]
- **Lenovo ThinkPad P1 Gen 7:** Up to NVIDIA RTX 3000 Ada (8 GB GDDR6) or a consumer GeForce RTX 4070; the high-end RTX 4000/5000 Ada options from the previous generation are no longer offered [https://www.notebookcheck.net/Lenovo-ThinkPad-P1-Gen-7-review-Without-TrackPoint-buttons-with-Nvidia-GeForce-RTX-4060.901578.0.html]

**Rendering Implications**
- **Lumion** is primarily GPU-bound and benefits directly from CUDA core count, VRAM capacity, and memory bandwidth. 4K rasterization requires at least 8 GB VRAM; ray-traced 1080p also requires 8 GB minimum [https://lumion.com/product/system-requirements]. The RTX 3000 Ada (8 GB) in the Lenovo is therefore at the minimum threshold, whereas the RTX 5000 Ada (16 GB) in the Dell and HP provides substantial headroom for large scenes and ray-traced outputs.
- In real-world viz benchmarks, an RTX 4000 Ada desktop card rendered Lumion scenes roughly 25–32% faster than its predecessor, and the RTX 2000 Ada delivered batch renders 27–37% faster than the prior Ampere generation [https://develop3d.com/hardware/review-nvidia-rtx-4000-ada-generation/] [https://www.bina-i.com/review-nvidia-rtx-2000-ada-generation/].
- **Bottom line:** For GPU-heavy Lumion production, the Dell and HP can be configured with roughly twice the raw compute and double the VRAM of the Lenovo. AutoCAD and Revit modeling remain somewhat single-thread CPU dependent, but Revit rendering and real-time visualization scale strongly with the GPU options where the HP and Dell hold a decisive advantage.

---

### 2. Thermal Management Under Sustained CAD Loads in Warm Environments

Dubai’s ambient temperatures and sustained architectural workloads make thermal behavior a critical longevity and comfort factor.

**Measured Thermal Behavior**
- **Dell Precision 5690:** In sustained CPU stress (10–15 min), the Core Ultra 7 165H throttles to ~58 W at 72 °C after an initial spike to 91 °C under medium load [https://laptopmedia.com/au/review/dell-precision-5690-review-is-it-the-perfect-compact-workstation/]. The RTX 2000 Ada variant sustains ~89 W at 76 °C after 30 minutes. High-end SKUs add vapor-chamber cooling; lower SKUs use conventional heat pipes.
- **HP ZBook Fury 16 G11:** The thicker chassis (28.5 mm) and larger fan array keep performance consistent without severe throttling, but reviewers report the underside becomes “almost scalding hot” and fans “really started blasting” under heavy rendering [https://postperspective.com/review-hp-zbook-fury-16-g11-mobile-workstation/]. HP explicitly documents that **“system performance may be reduced above 32 °C (89.6 °F)”** ambient [https://www.lap4worx.de/media/pdf/3f/49/3c/HP-ZBook-Fury-G11-QuickSpecs.pdf]. This is a significant caveat for Dubai offices where summer ambient temperatures can approach or exceed that threshold.
- **Lenovo ThinkPad P1 Gen 7:** In “Best Performance” mode, stress tests recorded CPU temperatures up to **103 °C** and GPU temperatures up to **88 °C** [https://www.notebookcheck.net/Lenovo-ThinkPad-P1-Gen-7-review-Without-TrackPoint-buttons-with-Nvidia-GeForce-RTX-4060.901578.0.html]. The thinner chassis sacrifices thermal headroom, and multiple sources note it “runs hot under prolonged heavy workloads” [https://www.seriousinsights.net/lenovo-thinkpad-p1-gen-7/].

**Warm-Environment Verdict**
- The HP’s size advantage allows it to dissipate heat more effectively, yet its official thermal policy throttles performance above 32 °C ambient—an unavoidable risk in the UAE.
- The Dell runs slightly cooler than the Lenovo under sustained load but its compact form factor still pushes CPU packages into the low 90 °C range before settling.
- The Lenovo runs the hottest and is most prone to thermal discomfort and long-term component stress in warm rooms.

---

### 3. RAM Expandability Toward 128 GB

Future-proofing for large Revit models, point clouds, and concurrent applications requires memory headroom.

| Model | Max RAM | Slots/Type | User Upgradeable? | 128 GB Feasible? |
|-------|---------|------------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Dell Precision 5690** | 64 GB | Soldered LPDDR5x-7467 | **No** | **No** |
| **HP ZBook Fury 16 G11** | 128 GB | 4× SODIMM DDR5-5600 (ECC optional) | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| **Lenovo ThinkPad P1 Gen 7** | 64 GB | 1× LPCAMM2 (LPDDR5X-7500) | Yes (module replaceable) | **No** |

**Sources:**
- Dell owner’s manual confirms memory is “integrated on system board,” “not replaceable or upgradeable,” with a 64 GB ceiling [https://www.dell.com/support/manuals/en-us/precision-16-5690-laptop/precision-5690_owners_manual/memory]
- HP specifications list 4 SODIMM slots and configurations up to 128 GB DDR5-5600 ECC [https://support.hp.com/us-en/document/ish_10384629-10384675-16]
- Lenovo PSREF specifies one LPCAMM2 connector, dual-channel, “up to 64 GB (1× 64 GB LPCAMM2)” [https://psrefstuff.lenovo.com/syspool/Sys/PDF/ThinkPad/ThinkPad_P1_Gen_7/ThinkPad_P1_Gen_7_Spec.pdf]

**Bottom line:** Only the HP ZBook Fury 16 G11 can scale to 128 GB. The Dell and Lenovo cap at 64 GB; because the Dell’s RAM is soldered, the configuration chosen at purchase is permanent. For an 8-user firm working with BIM and large assets, the HP’s quad-SODIMM design provides the only viable path beyond 64 GB.

---

### 4. Enterprise Support Options in the UAE

All three OEMs maintain UAE business support channels, but service tiers differ.

**Dell**
- ProSupport and **ProSupport Plus** available in UAE: 24×7 hardware/software support, next-business-day (NBD) onsite repair, accidental damage, hard-drive retention, and extended battery service [https://www.dell.com/support/contents/en-ae/article/warranty/prosupport-suite-for-pcs]
- UAE business support lines: 8000 441 3889 (business hours) and 8000 441 3745 (24×7 English) [https://www.dell.com/support/incidents-online/en-ae/contactus/Dynamic]

**HP**
- Sold heavily through UAE partners (Al Ershad, LastBestPrice) with 1-year or 3-year warranty bundles. HP Care Packs can upgrade to 3–5 years with NBD onsite and battery replacement options [https://lastbestprice.com/shop/hp/hp-zbook-fury-16-inch-g11-mobile-workstation-9c597av/]
- Accidental damage is typically an add-on rather than bundled in the top tier.

**Lenovo**
- **Premier Support Plus** offers 24×7×365 support, NBD onsite prioritization in select markets, and single-point-of-contact case management [https://www.lenovo.com/ae/en/premier-support-plus/]
- Local UAE Premier Support includes Arabic and English coverage.

**Practical Note for Dubai:** Dell and Lenovo both publish direct UAE enterprise support pages, while HP relies more on its authorized reseller/service-partner network. For an 8-seat firm, Dell’s ProSupport Plus and Lenovo’s Premier Support Plus offer the most predictable escalation paths with dedicated account management at scale.

---

### 5. Five-Year Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)

**Acquisition Cost (UAE market, representative mid/high configs)**
- **Dell Precision 5690:** Core Ultra 7, 32 GB, RTX 2000 Ada, 3-yr warranty ≈ **AED 13,513–13,860** [https://lastbestprice.com/shop/dell/dell-mobile-precision-5690-workstation-intel-core-ultra-7-165h-3yr/]
- **HP ZBook Fury 16 G11:** Core i9-14900HX, 32 GB, RTX 3500 Ada, 3-yr warranty ≈ **AED 17,140** [https://lastbestprice.com/shop/hp/hp-zbook-fury-16-inch-g11-mobile-workstation-9c597av/]
- **Lenovo ThinkPad P1 Gen 7:** Core Ultra 9, 64 GB, RTX 2000 Ada, 3-yr warranty ≈ **AED 13,289** [https://www.alershadonline.com/collections/mobile-workstation/products/lenovo-thinkpad-p1-gen-7-mobile-workstation-intel-core-ultra-9-185h-processor-64gb-ram-1tb-ssd-nvidia-rtx-2000-ada-8gb-graphics-16-wqxga-165hz-display-windows-11-pro-backlit-english-arabic-keyboard-3-year-warranty-21kv0024gr]

**Major TCO Levers Over 5 Years**

1. **Warranty Extension vs. Out-of-Pocket Failures**
   - Industry models suggest that keeping notebooks in years 4 and 5 adds failure costs equivalent to a large fraction of the device purchase price [https://i.dell.com/sites/content/business/smb/sb360/en/documents/wp-nb-failures-costs.pdf]. Dell estimates the distributed failure cost per machine at roughly **$422 in year 4 and $538 in year 5**. A 3–5 year ProSupport or Care Pack contract mitigates unplanned repair costs.

2. **Battery Lifecycle & Replacement**
   - Dell’s own planning whitepaper estimates **~300 charge/discharge cycles** as normal battery life and expects roughly **50% of fleet batteries to need replacement annually starting in year 2** [https://i.dell.com/sites/content/business/smb/sb360/en/documents/wp-nb-failures-costs.pdf].
   - HP batteries since 2017 carry warranty aligned to the device or 1,000 cycles, whichever comes first [https://support.hp.com/us-en/document/ish_7684705-7684749-16].
   - **For a 5-year lifecycle, assume at least one, and likely two, battery replacements per machine.** Dell’s Extended Battery Service extends coverage up to year 4; HP offers a battery-only Care Pack. Lenovo Premier Support can include battery coverage. Over five years, budgeting **AED 400–700 per machine for battery swaps** is prudent.

3. **Configuration Rigidity vs. Upgrade Path**
   - The Dell and Lenovo 64 GB RAM ceilings mean that if project complexity grows, those machines must be retired early or users must tolerate memory pressure. The HP’s 128 GB SODIMM slots defer replacement by 1–2 years, improving amortized TCO even though the upfront unit price is higher.
   - Similarly, the Lenovo’s top GPU (RTX 3000 Ada/RTX 4070) is mid-tier compared with the HP/Dell ceiling (RTX 5000 Ada). If Lumion ray-traced workflows expand, the Lenovo fleet may face earlier obsolescence.

4. **Thermal Climate Depreciation**
   - Dubai’s warm ambient conditions accelerate battery degradation and increase fan/dust wear. Machines that throttle less and run cooler (HP Fury’s larger heatsink, Dell’s vapor chamber on high SKUs) generally exhibit lower failure rates over time than thin chassis designs (Lenovo P1) that sustain 100 °C+ junction temperatures.

**Estimated 5-Year TCO Ranking (Lowest to Highest)**
1. **Lenovo ThinkPad P1 Gen 7:** Lowest entry price, but highest risk of early obsolescence due to 64 GB RAM ceiling and mid-tier GPU cap. Thermal stress in warm environments may elevate failure rates in years 3–5.
2. **Dell Precision 5690:** Mid-range TCO. Soldered 64 GB RAM is a hard cap, but GPU options up to RTX 5000 Ada extend viz relevance. Vapor chamber on high SKUs improves longevity.
3. **HP ZBook Fury 16 G11:** Highest upfront unit cost (~20–30% premium over comparably equipped rivals), but the only platform with a **true 128 GB RAM upgrade path** and desktop-class HX CPUs. For users who will outgrow 64 GB within 3–4 years, the HP likely delivers the **lowest 5-year TCO per productive seat**.

---

### Summary Recommendation

| Factor | Recommended Platform |
|--------|---------------------|
| **Maximum GPU rendering (Lumion/VR)** | Dell Precision 5690 or HP ZBook Fury 16 G11 (both offer RTX 5000 Ada) |
| **Warm-climate sustained CAD loads** | HP ZBook Fury 16 G11 (thicker chassis, better sustained clocks), though note HP’s >32 °C ambient throttling policy |
| **RAM expandability to 128 GB** | **HP ZBook Fury 16 G11 exclusively** |
| **Enterprise support in UAE** | Dell and Lenovo have the most transparent direct-to-business UAE portals; HP is strong via authorized partners |
| **Lowest 5-year TCO for growing workloads** | **HP ZBook Fury 16 G11** (upgradeability defers replacement) |
| **Lowest acquisition cost today** | Lenovo ThinkPad P1 Gen 7 |

**Final Assessment:** For an 8-designer firm running AutoCAD, Revit, and Lumion in Dubai, the **HP ZBook Fury 16 G11** is the most defensible long-term investment despite its higher initial price, because it is the only unit among the three that can scale to 128 GB RAM and accommodates the highest-tier RTX Ada GPUs in a thermally adequate chassis. The **Dell Precision 5690** is a viable alternative for users prioritizing portability and strong GPU performance, provided 64 GB RAM is sufficient for the full lifecycle. The **Lenovo ThinkPad P1 Gen 7** is best suited to lighter-weight CAD users or executives who value thinness over maximum rendering throughput.

python3 -c "
# Post-tax yield calculations for NCDs
# Tax slabs (Old Regime for comparison, as many seniors opt for it)
# Generic investor (under 60): Basic exemption 2.5L, then 5%, 20%, 30%
# Senior citizen (60-79): Basic exemption 3L, then 5%, 20%, 30%
# Super senior (80+): Basic exemption 5L, then 20%, 30%

# For NCD interest income, it's taxed at slab rate
# Let's calculate post-tax yields for different income levels

print('=== POST-TAX YIELD ANALYSIS ===')
print()

# Muthoot Mercantile NCD - highest yield 11.73% (75 months cumulative)
# Edelweiss Financial - highest yield 10.09% (120 months annual)

yields = {
    'Muthoot Mercantile (75m cumulative)': 11.73,
    'Muthoot Mercantile (60m monthly)': 11.30,
    'Muthoot Mercantile (36m monthly)': 10.75,
    'Edelweiss Financial (120m annual)': 10.09,
    'Edelweiss Financial (60m annual)': 9.85,
    'Edelweiss Financial (36m monthly)': 9.40,
}

# Tax rates for different investor profiles at various income levels
# Assuming interest income is the primary/only income source for retirees

print('Post-Tax Effective Yields (%)')
print('-' * 80)
print(f'{'Instrument':<45} {'Pre-Tax':>8} {'Gen@5%':>8} {'Gen@20%':>8} {'Gen@30%':>8} {'Sr@5%':>8} {'Sr@20%':>8} {'Sr@30%':>8}')
print('-' * 80)

for name, pre_tax in yields.items():
    gen_5 = pre_tax * 0.95
    gen_20 = pre_tax * 0.80
    gen_30 = pre_tax * 0.70
    sr_5 = pre_tax * 0.95
    sr_20 = pre_tax * 0.80
    sr_30 = pre_tax * 0.70
    print(f'{name:<45} {pre_tax:>8.2f} {gen_5:>8.2f} {gen_20:>8.2f} {gen_30:>8.2f} {sr_5:>8.2f} {sr_20:>8.2f} {sr_30:>8.2f}')

print()
print('Note: Tax rates applied on interest income at slab rates')
print('Senior citizens (60+) get basic exemption of Rs 3L (old regime) vs 2.5L for general')
print('Super seniors (80+) get basic exemption of Rs 5L')
print('Section 80TTB allows deduction of Rs 50,000 (FY25) / Rs 1,00,000 (FY26) on interest income for seniors in old regime')
print()

# With 80TTB benefit for senior citizens
print('=== WITH SECTION 80TTB DEDUCTION (Old Regime) ===')
print('For a senior citizen with Rs 5 lakh interest income:')
print('Taxable after 80TTB (FY25): 5,00,000 - 50,000 = 4,50,000 -> Tax = 7,500 (5% slab)')
print('Effective tax rate = 7,500/5,00,000 = 1.5%')
print('For a senior citizen with Rs 10 lakh interest income:')
print('Taxable after 80TTB (FY25): 10,00,000 - 50,000 = 9,50,000 -> Tax = 92,500 (5% + 20% slab)')
print('Effective tax rate = 92,500/10,00,000 = 9.25%')
print()
print('For a senior citizen with Rs 5 lakh interest income (FY26, 80TTB=1L):')
print('Taxable after 80TTB: 5,00,000 - 1,00,000 = 4,00,000 -> Tax = 5,000 (5% slab)')
print('Effective tax rate = 5,000/5,00,000 = 1.0%')
print('For a senior citizen with Rs 10 lakh interest income (FY26, 80TTB=1L):')
print('Taxable after 80TTB: 10,00,000 - 1,00,000 = 9,00,000 -> Tax = 82,500')
print('Effective tax rate = 82,500/10,00,000 = 8.25%')
"

final.content:
**INDIA NCD PUBLIC ISSUES – DECEMBER 2025**
**Fixed Income Portfolio Analysis for Retiree & Institutional Mandates**
*Research Date: December 8, 2025*

---

### EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

As of December 8, 2025, two public NCD (Non-Convertible Debenture) issues are open for subscription in India: (1) **Muthoot Mercantile Limited** (closes December 17, 2025) and (2) **Edelweiss Financial Services Limited** (closes December 18, 2025). A third issue from **Adani Enterprises Limited** is filed but opens January 6, 2026, and is therefore not yet available.

For retiree and fixed-income mandates, the central tension is yield versus credit quality. Muthoot Mercantile offers effective yields up to **11.73%** but carries an **IND BBB/Stable** rating and a pro-forma debt-to-equity ratio of **5.19x**. Edelweiss Financial offers effective yields up to **10.09%** with a **CRISIL A+/Stable** rating and consolidated gearing of approximately **3.02x–4.18x** depending on the metric. Neither issuer matches sovereign safety, and both operate in an NBFC sector where funding costs, gold-loan delinquencies, and regulatory tightening present active headwinds.

---

### 1. OPEN NCD ISSUES: TERMS AND STRUCTURE

#### 1.1 Muthoot Mercantile Limited
| Parameter | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Open / Close** | December 4, 2025 / December 17, 2025 |
| **Issue Size** | Base ₹75 Cr; Green Shoe to ₹150 Cr |
| **Face Value** | ₹1,000 per NCD |
| **Minimum Application** | 10 NCDs (₹10,000) |
| **Credit Rating** | **IND BBB/Stable** (India Ratings & Research) |
| **Listing** | BSE |
| **Security** | Secured; first ranking pari passu charge on specified movable assets |
| **Use of Proceeds** | ≥75% onward lending / repayment of borrowings; ≤25% general corporate |
| **Debenture Trustee** | Mitcon Credentia Trusteeship Services Ltd |

**Coupon Schedule (Selected Series):**
| Series | Tenor | Payment | Coupon | Effective Yield |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| I | 400 Days | Cumulative | NA | 9.71% |
| II | 400 Days | Monthly | 9.50% | 9.92% |
| VI | 36 Months | Monthly | 10.25% | 10.75% |
| VIII | 60 Months | Monthly | 10.75% | 11.30% |
| IX | 75 Months | Cumulative | NA | **11.73%** |

*Sources: IPO Watch (https://ipowatch.in/muthoot-mercantile-ncd-december-2025/); Chittorgarh NCD Review (https://www.chittorgarh.com/ncd_review/muthoot-mercantile-ncd-december-2025/4878/); Stockholding Prospectus extract (https://online.stockholding.com/ipo/old_file/MMLNCDEDDEC_PN.pdf)*

#### 1.2 Edelweiss Financial Services Limited
| Parameter | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Open / Close** | December 5, 2025 / December 18, 2025 |
| **Issue Size** | Base ₹125 Cr; Green Shoe to ₹250 Cr |
| **Face Value** | ₹1,000 per NCD |
| **Minimum Application** | 10 NCDs (₹10,000) |
| **Credit Rating** | **CRISIL A+/Stable** (Crisil Ratings Limited) |
| **Listing** | BSE |
| **Security** | Secured redeemable NCDs |
| **Use of Proceeds** | ≥75% repayment/prepayment of existing borrowings; ≤25% general corporate |
| **Debenture Trustee** | To be appointed per SEBI norms |

**Coupon Schedule (Selected Series):**
| Series | Tenor | Payment | Coupon | Effective Yield |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| I | 24 Months | Annual | 8.85% | 8.85% |
| III | 36 Months | Monthly | 9.03% | 9.40% |
| VI | 60 Months | Monthly | 9.44% | 9.86% |
| VII | 60 Months | Annual | 9.85% | 9.84% |
| X | 120 Months | Annual | 10.10% | **10.09%** |

*Sources: RR Finance (https://www.rrfinance.com/OurProducts/PublicIssues/Edelweiss_Financial_Services_Limited_NCD_dec25.aspx); IPO Watch (https://ipowatch.in/edelweiss-financial-services-ncd-december-2025/); Chittorgarh NCD Review (https://www.chittorgarh.com/ncd_review/edelweiss-financial-services-ncd-december-2025/4881/)*

---

### 2. CREDIT RATINGS: INTERPRETATION AND RELATIVE VALUE

| Issuer | Rating Agency | Rating | Outlook | Implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Muthoot Mercantile | India Ratings | IND BBB | Stable | Moderate degree of safety; moderate credit risk; lowest investment-grade tier preferred by institutional investors |
| Edelweiss Financial | CRISIL | A+ | Stable | Adequate safety; lower credit risk than BBB; stronger capacity to meet obligations |

**Key Distinction:** A **BBB** rating is the threshold of investment grade. Historical precedent in India (DHFL, IL&FS) demonstrates that BBB-rated NBFCs can experience rapid downgrades to default during liquidity stress. **A+** provides a thicker cushion, though it is still non-sovereign credit risk.

*Source: Chittorgarh NCD Review for Muthoot (https://www.chittorgarh.com/ncd_review/muthoot-mercantile-ncd-december-2025/4878/); RR Finance for Edelweiss (https://www.rrfinance.com/OurProducts/PublicIssues/Edelweiss_Financial_Services_Limited_NCD_dec25.aspx)*

---

### 3. POST-TAX YIELD ANALYSIS: GENERIC INVESTOR VS. SENIOR CITIZEN

NCD interest is taxed as **"Income from Other Sources"** at the investor’s applicable slab rate. Accrued interest is taxable annually even on cumulative options.

#### 3.1 Tax Framework (Assessment Year 2026-27)
| Investor Type | Basic Exemption (Old Regime) | Section 80TTB Deduction (Interest Income) |
|---|---|---|
| General (<60 years) | ₹2,50,000 | Not applicable |
| Senior Citizen (60–79 years) | ₹3,00,000 | ₹50,000 (FY 2025-26); ₹1,00,000 (FY 2026-27) |
| Super Senior (80+ years) | ₹5,00,000 | ₹50,000 / ₹1,00,000 |

**TDS Thresholds:** For company NCDs, TDS under Section 193/194A typically applies when interest from a single issuer exceeds **₹5,000** in a financial year (10% with PAN; 20% without). Budget 2025 raised bank FD thresholds to ₹50,000 (general) and ₹1,00,000 (seniors), but NCD-specific thresholds remain lower unless the issuer structures payments to fall within the revised 194A limits. Investors should file **Form 15H** (seniors) or **15G** (others) if total income is below taxable limits to avoid TDS.

*Sources: ClearTax NCD Guide (https://cleartax.in/s/non-convertible-debentures-ncd); Income Tax Department slabs (https://incometaxindia.gov.in/Tutorials/2%20Tax%20Rates.pdf); BondDekho (https://www.bonddekho.com/blog/bond-interest-taxation-india); HDFC Bank Tax Blog (https://www.hdfc.bank.in/blogs/nri-banking/know-the-various-tax-on-interest-incomes)*

#### 3.2 Post-Tax Yield Scenarios

The following table shows effective post-tax yields across slab rates:

| Instrument | Pre-Tax Yield | @ 5% Slab | @ 20% Slab | @ 30% Slab |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Muthoot Mercantile (75m, Cumulative) | 11.73% | 11.14% | 9.38% | 8.21% |
| Muthoot Mercantile (60m, Monthly) | 11.30% | 10.73% | 9.04% | 7.91% |
| Muthoot Mercantile (36m, Monthly) | 10.75% | 10.21% | 8.60% | 7.52% |
| Edelweiss Financial (120m, Annual) | 10.09% | 9.59% | 8.07% | 7.06% |
| Edelweiss Financial (60m, Annual) | 9.85% | 9.36% | 7.88% | 6.89% |
| Edelweiss Financial (36m, Monthly) | 9.40% | 8.93% | 7.52% | 6.58% |

**Senior Citizen Advantage:** A senior citizen in the old regime with, for example, ₹5 lakh of interest income and claiming the enhanced **₹1,00,000 Section 80TTB** deduction (effective FY 2026-27) would have taxable income of ₹4,00,000, attracting only **₹5,000** tax (1.0% effective rate on the interest). Thus, a retiree in the lowest tax brackets can retain nearly the full coupon. Under the new regime, a **Section 87A rebate of up to ₹60,000** wipes out tax liability for total income up to ₹12 lakh, making NCD interest effectively tax-free for small-to-moderate portfolios.

*Sources: BusinessToday on new regime rebate (https://www.businesstoday.in/personal-finance/tax/story/is-interest-income-taxable-under-new-tax-regime-for-senior-citizens-key-details-here-486445-2025-07-26); Finlecture on 80TTB (https://finlecture.in/indian-tax-system/income-tax-for-senior-citizens-fy-2026-27/)*

---

### 4. FINANCIAL METRICS AND DEBT-TO-EQUITY ANALYSIS

#### 4.1 Muthoot Mercantile Limited — HIGH LEVERAGE FLAG
| Metric | Value | Date |
|---|---|---|
| Total Income (H1 FY26) | ₹108.74 Cr | Sept 30, 2025 |
| PAT (H1 FY26) | ₹22.73 Cr | Sept 30, 2025 |
| Total Income (FY25) | ₹166.43 Cr | March 31, 2025 |
| PAT (FY25) | ₹28.09 Cr | March 31, 2025 |
| Total Assets | ₹1,211 Cr | Sept 30, 2025 |
| Net Worth | ₹216.35 Cr | Sept 30, 2025 |
| **Debt-to-Equity Ratio** | **4.48x** | **Sept 30, 2025** |
| **Pro-Forma D/E (Post-Issue)** | **5.19x** | **Post-Dec 2025** |
| Net NPA | 0.39% | Sept 30, 2025 |
| Gross NPA | 0.59% | Sept 30, 2025 |
| Capital Adequacy Ratio (CAR) | ~21% | Sept 30, 2025 |
| Gold Loan AUM | ₹1,064.36 Cr (99.61% of loans) | Sept 30, 2025 |
| Branches | 317 | Oct 31, 2025 |

**Red Flag:** A debt-to-equity ratio of **4.48x**, rising to **5.19x** post-issue, is elevated even for a gold-loan NBFC. While gold collateral provides a buffer, the leverage implies thin equity cushion against asset-quality shocks. The company has already issued NCDs **seven times since December 2023**, indicating heavy reliance on retail market funding.

*Sources: Chittorgarh NCD Review (https://www.chittorgarh.com/ncd_review/muthoot-mercantile-ncd-december-2025/4878/); Muthoot Prospectus/Abridged Financials (https://muthootenterprises.com/pdf/Investors/Abridged%20Prospectus.pdf); Stockholding PN extract (https://online.stockholding.com/ipo/old_file/MMLNCDEDDEC_PN.pdf)*

#### 4.2 Edelweiss Financial Services Limited
| Metric | Value | Date |
|---|---|---|
| Consolidated Revenue (FY25) | ₹9,519 Cr | March 31, 2025 |
| Consolidated PAT (FY25) | ₹535.82 Cr | March 31, 2025 |
| Net Worth | ₹5,918 Cr | March 31, 2025 |
| Net Debt | ₹11,170 Cr | March 31, 2025 |
| **Debt-to-Equity (Company Definition)** | **3.02x** | **March 31, 2025** |
| **Gross Debt / Tangible Net Worth (CARE)** | **4.18x** | **March 31, 2025** |
| Interest Coverage Ratio | 1.32x | March 31, 2025 |
| Liquidity Buffer | ₹4,255 Cr (28% of debt) | March 31, 2025 |
| AUM (Mutual Fund) | ₹1,418 Bn | March 31, 2025 |
| Offices (Domestic + International) | 255 | Sept 30, 2025 |

Edelweiss is a diversified financial conglomerate with asset management, insurance, and credit businesses. While consolidated leverage is high, the group has reduced net debt by **27% YoY** and maintains a liquidity buffer sufficient for **~7–8 months** of obligations without incremental fundraising. The interest coverage of **1.32x** is adequate but not generous.

*Sources: Edelweiss FY25 Annual Report (https://cdn1.edelweissfin.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/Edelweiss-Financial-Services-Annual-Report-FY25-1.pdf); Q4FY25 Earnings Update (https://cdn1.edelweissfin.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/Financials-and-Earnings-Update-Q4FY25.pdf); CARE Rating Rationale (https://www.careratings.com/upload/CompanyFiles/PR/202511141145_Edelweiss_Financial_Services_Limited.pdf); CRISIL Rating Rationale (https://www.crisilratings.com/mnt/winshare/Ratings/RatingList/RatingDocs/EdelweissFinancialServicesLimited_January%2009_%202026_RR_385951.html)*

---

### 5. HISTORICAL PRECEDENTS AND PATTERN RECOGNITION

#### 5.1 The IL&FS and DHFL Crises (2018–2020)
- **IL&FS (Sept 2018):** Defaulted on ~₹91,000 Cr of debt. The group had complex subsidiaries and was rated investment grade until weeks before collapse. The event triggered an NBFC liquidity freeze, with mutual funds pulling out of commercial paper and NCDs.
- **DHFL (2019–2020):** Rated **AAA** by CARE and Brickworks until January 2019, then downgraded to **D (Default)** within three months of first missed payments. DHFL defaulted on **₹50 Cr of secured NCDs** in July 2020. Retail NCD holders ultimately faced **~60% haircuts** (secured) and FD holders **~75%** losses in the Piramal resolution plan.

**Pattern:** High leverage, rapid balance-sheet expansion, and reliance on market borrowings preceded both defaults. Credit ratings proved lagging indicators. Retail investors in long-tenor NCDs suffered because secondary markets froze and recovery via IBC was protracted.

*Sources: CAFRAL Report on IL&FS/DHFL (https://www.cafral.org.in/sfControl/content/NewsEvent/CAFRAL_Report.pdf); Financial Express on DHFL NCD default (https://www.financialexpress.com/business/industry-dhfl-defaults-on-ncd-repayments-of-rs-50-cr-says-cant-pay-due-to-moratorium-restrictions-2013854/); MoneyLife on DHFL recovery (https://www.moneylife.in/article/dhfls-fd-and-ncdholders-may-lose-twice-over-unless-they-intervene/62641/index.html); Business Standard on IL&FS aftermath (https://www.business-standard.com/article/finance/il-fs-crisis-for-nbfcs-india-s-lehman-moment-has-meant-reset-of-future-121022501525_1.html)*

#### 5.2 Gold Loan Sector Stress Signals (2025)
TransUnion CIBIL data for H1 2025 shows:
- Overall gold loan delinquency: **1.1%**
- Borrowers with >₹2.5 lakh outstanding: **1.5% delinquency** (2.2x higher than lower exposures)
- Borrowers with >5 gold loans: **1.9% delinquency**
- **48%** of gold-loan borrowers now have >₹2.5 lakh outstanding, indicating rising leverage.

This is directly relevant to Muthoot Mercantile, whose portfolio is **99.6% gold loans**.

*Sources: Economic Times (https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/industry/banking/finance/gold-loan-delinquency-rates-send-red-signal/articleshow/130268019.cms); BusinessToday (https://www.businesstoday.in/personal-finance/news/story/as-gold-loans-surge-so-have-delinquencies-among-borrowers-with-bigger-amounts-finds-study-525658-2026-04-15)*

---

### 6. COMPETITIVE POSITIONING AND RELATIVE VALUE

| Benchmark | Rate (Approx.) | Tenor | Safety |
|---|---|---|---|
| Senior Citizen Savings Scheme (SCSS) | **8.20%** | 5 years | Sovereign (zero credit risk) |
| SBI Senior Citizen FD | 6.80–7.05% | 5 years | Bank + DICGC ₹5L cover |
| Major Pvt Bank Senior FD | 7.00–7.50% | 5 years | Bank + DICGC |
| Post Office 5-Year FD | 7.50% | 5 years | Sovereign |
| **Edelweiss NCD (A+)** | **8.85–10.09%** | 2–10 years | Secured; NBFC credit risk |
| **Muthoot NCD (BBB)** | **9.71–11.73%** | 400d–75m | Secured; gold collateral; higher risk |

**Spread Analysis:**
- Muthoot 75-month yield (11.73%) vs. SCSS (8.20%) = **+353 bps** credit spread.
- Edelweiss 120-month yield (10.09%) vs. SCSS = **+189 bps** credit spread.

For a retiree, the question is whether the spread compensates for the risk of permanent capital impairment. Given the DHFL precedent, **353 bps for a BBB-rated, 5.19x leveraged gold-loan NBFC is arguably insufficient** for a core retirement allocation.

*Sources: Economic Times on SCSS (https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/wealth/invest/latest-senior-citizen-savings-scheme-interest-rate-what-is-the-post-office-scss-rate-for-april-june-2025-quarter/articleshow/119906513.cms); Financial Express on Post Office vs Bank FDs (https://www.financialexpress.com/money/post-office-schemes-vs-bank-fds-these-govt-schemes-offer-higher-interest-than-most-major-banks-check-rates-3902964/)*

---

### 7. COMPREHENSIVE RISK ASSESSMENT

#### 7.1 Credit Risk
- **Muthoot:** BBB rating with stable outlook. India Ratings notes "moderate degree of safety." The 7th NCD in 24 months signals funding dependence. Pro-forma D/E of **5.19x** leaves limited buffer. If gold prices correct and LTVs breach regulatory limits, forced auctions could accelerate.
- **Edelweiss:** A+ rating with stable outlook. Diversified revenue streams reduce single-sector concentration. However, consolidated gross debt/TNW of **4.18x** and interest coverage of **1.32x** mean the group is not immune to a prolonged credit cycle downturn.

#### 7.2 Liquidity and Market Risk
- **Secondary Market Illiquidity:** Public NCDs list on BSE, but trading volumes are typically thin. A retiree needing emergency exit may face wide bid-ask spreads or no buyers.
- **ALM Mismatch:** NBFCs historically borrow short (NCDs/CP) and lend long. RBI has tightened ALM norms post-IL&FS, but refinancing risk remains for issuers.

#### 7.3 Sectoral and Tail Risks
- **Gold Loan Moderation:** ICRA expects gold-loan growth to slow as the price-driven surge normalizes. Muthoot’s AUM growth (28% CAGR) may decelerate, pressuring earnings.
- **Lower-Rated NBFC Squeeze:** India Ratings (May 2025) explicitly flagged that **A and BBB-rated NBFCs face sharper slowdowns**, elevated funding costs, and asset-quality stress than higher-rated peers.
- **Regulatory Tail Risk:** RBI’s June 2025 revised gold-loan directions changed LTV computation for bullet loans to include accrued interest. While Crisil estimates this only raises disbursement LTV to 70–75%, any future tightening could constrain Muthoot’s core business.

*Sources: RBI Trend and Progress Report (https://www.rbi.org.in/Scripts/PublicationsView.aspx?id=23641); ICRA NBFC Outlook (https://www.icra.in/Rating/DownloadResearchSummaryReport?id=6314); Business Standard on lower-rated NBFC stress (https://www.business-standard.com/industry/banking/nbfcs-rated-a-and-bbb-face-slowdown-on-asset-quality-funding-woes-125050800707_1.html); FIDC Newsletter on RBI gold loan norms (https://www.fidcindia.org.in/wp-content/uploads/2025/08/FIDC-NEWSLETTER-VOL.17-NO.-2.pdf)*

---

### 8. REGULATORY LANDSCAPE AND POLICY IMPLICATIONS

- **SEBI NCS Regulations 2021:** Govern public issuance, mandatory listing, debenture trustee oversight, and disclosure. Security must be registered with CERSAI/Sub-Registrar or independently verifiable.
- **RBI Master Direction – NBFC Scale Based Regulation 2023 (updated 2025):** Consolidates norms on private placement NCDs (minimum ₹20,000 subscription; 200-subscriber limit for retail private placements). Public issues are exempt from the 200-subscriber cap but require full SEBI compliance.
- **Debenture Trustee Accountability:** SEBI Master Circular (May 2024) mandates trustees to verify security creation and monitor covenant compliance. Investors should confirm security registration before subscribing.
- **Tax Policy:** Budget 2025 raised TDS thresholds for bank interest and enhanced Section 87A rebate (new regime), but NCD interest remains fully taxable at slab. The FY 2026-27 enhancement of **Section 80TTB to ₹1,00,000** benefits senior citizens in the old regime.

*Sources: RBI Master Directions (https://www.rbi.org.in/scripts/BS_ViewMasDirections.aspx?id=12931); SEBI Debenture Trustee Master Circular via CDSL (https://www.cdslindia.com/downloads/Publications/Communique/DP-333-SEBI-MASTER-CIR-Debenture-Trustees.pdf); TaxGuru on NBFC NCD Framework (https://taxguru.in/rbi/issuance-ncds-nbfcs-regulatory-framework-guide.html)*

---

### 9. FORWARD CURVE AND LEADING INDICATORS

- **Interest Rate Trajectory:** RBI cut the repo rate by **100 bps** during 2025. Bank FD rates have fallen sharply (SBI 5-year senior FD now ~7%). This makes NCD coupons appear relatively attractive, but it also means **reinvestment risk** for cumulative options if rates stay lower for longer.
- **NBFC Funding Costs:** ICRA expects weighted average cost of funds for NBFCs to decline by **10–30 bps** in FY2026. This could support issuer profitability but does not eliminate credit risk.
- **Gold Price Outlook:** Gold loans surged 50–60% in recent years, driven largely by price appreciation rather than volume. Unless gold prices rise further, AUM growth will moderate. Any price correction threatens collateral coverage.
- **Sector Growth:** ICRA forecasts overall NBFC credit growth to moderate to **16–17%** in FY2027 from the post-pandemic surge. Lower-rated entities will feel the pinch first.

*Sources: Financial Express on NBFC moderation (https://www.financialexpress.com/business/banking-finance-nbfc-growth-may-moderate-as-gold-loan-boom-untenable-icra-4168574/); EY Private Credit India H2 2025 (https://www.ey.com/content/dam/ey-unified-site/ey-com/en-in/insights/strategy-transactions/documents/2026/ey-private-credit-in-india-h2-2025-update.pdf)*

---

### 10. INVESTMENT THESIS AND RECOMMENDATIONS

#### For Retiree / Senior Citizen Mandates

**Primary Objective:** Capital preservation with inflation-beating, predictable income.

**Thesis:**
1. **Neither NCD offers sovereign safety.** The SCSS (8.20%) and Post Office 5-Year FD (7.50%) remain the benchmarks for core retirement allocations.
2. **Edelweiss Financial (A+/Stable)** is the **only currently open issue suitable for modest allocation** within a diversified fixed-income portfolio. The 10.09% yield (120-month tenor) provides a ~190 bps spread over SCSS. However, the 120-month duration is excessive for most retirees; the **36-month monthly series at 9.40% effective yield** offers a better duration-risk trade-off. Maximum allocation should be capped at **5–10% of fixed-income corpus** given NBFC sector concentration.
3. **Muthoot Mercantile (BBB/Stable)** should be **flagged as high risk** for conservative portfolios. The 11.73% yield is seductive, but the **5.19x pro-forma leverage**, 99.6% gold-loan concentration, rising delinquencies in the sector, and frequent return to the NCD market (7th issue in 24 months) suggest funding dependence and thin margin for error. If allocated at all, limit to **<3% of portfolio** and choose the shortest tenor (400 days / 24 months) to minimize duration risk.
4. **Tax Efficiency:** Senior citizens in the old regime with total interest income below ₹12 lakh should utilize **Section 80TTB (₹1 lakh deduction from FY26)** and basic exemptions to minimize effective tax. Those in the new regime with income under ₹12 lakh benefit from the enhanced **Section 87A rebate**, potentially paying zero tax. However, TDS may still be deducted at source; filing 15H is essential.

#### Red Flags for Investment Committee Review
- **Muthoot Mercantile:** Debt-to-equity of **5.19x** post-issue; BBB rating; 7th NCD since Dec 2023; gold-loan delinquency uptrend in sector.
- **Edelweiss Financial:** Consolidated gross debt/TNW of **4.18x**; interest coverage of **1.32x**; 18th NCD since Dec 2020.
- **Sector-Wide:** Lower-rated NBFCs face sharper slowdowns; gold-loan growth normalization; secondary market illiquidity for retail NCDs.

#### Compliance Note
This analysis is based on publicly available offer documents, rating rationales, and regulatory filings as of December 8, 2025. Investment committees should verify final RHP disclosures, security creation status with the debenture trustee, and any post-filing rating changes before finalizing subscriptions. Past defaults (DHFL, IL&FS) demonstrate that credit ratings can lag fundamentals; independent due diligence on issuer cash flows and security coverage is warranted.

---

*Report compiled from SEBI/RBI regulatory filings, CRISIL/India Ratings/CARE rating rationales, issuer prospectuses, and market data as of December 8, 2025.*

### (a) Market Size and Growth (2018–2024)

**Tonnage and Capacity**
The New Zealand structural steel fabrication sector has maintained relatively stable annual output through the period. According to Steel Construction New Zealand (SCNZ) quarterly fabricator forward-workload surveys, the industry turned approximately **100,000 tonnes** of structural steel into buildings and bridges in each of the years from 2018 through 2024. Total industry capacity is estimated at **120,000 tonnes per annum**, implying average utilization of roughly 83%. Spare capacity has fluctuated with demand cycles: **26%** (year ending June 2019), **40%** (June 2020, reflecting COVID-19 project cancellations of ~3.4% of annual volume and delays of ~7.4%), **37%** (March 2022), and **28%** (March 2024) [https://scnz.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/10/SCNZ_Industry-Update_September-2018.pdf, September 2018; https://scnz.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/09/SCNZ-IU-SEP2020-web.pdf, September 2020; https://scnz.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/03/SCNZ-IU-MAR-2021-Web.pdf, March 2021; https://scnz.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/03/SCNZ-IU-Mar-23.pdf, March 2023].

**Employment and Enterprise Counts**
Industry employment is approximately **5,000 workers** across the fabrication sector, per SCNZ [https://scnz.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/03/SCNZ-IU-Mar-23.pdf, March 2023]. Stats NZ Business Demography data (2022) records **4,450 employees** specifically in ANZSIC class C222100 *Structural Steel Fabricating*, within **291 enterprises** [https://statsnz.contentdm.oclc.org/digital/api/collection/p20045coll24/id/839/download, 2022]. The number of fabrication companies reported by SCNZ has grown from **86 nationwide** (2018) to **127** (2020–2022) and **153** (2023–2024), suggesting industry consolidation alongside new entrants [https://scnz.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/10/SCNZ_Industry-Update_September-2018.pdf, 2018; https://scnz.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/03/SCNZ-IU-Mar-23.pdf, March 2023].

**Revenue**
Official Stats NZ revenue series for the structural steel fabrication subclass are not publicly available in the gathered sources. IBISWorld forecasts the *Structural Steel Fabricating* industry will reach a market size of **$1.8 billion in 2026**, growing at a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of **2.1%** from 2021 to 2026 [https://www.ibisworld.com/new-zealand/industry/structural-steel-fabricating/235/, January 2026]. Back-casting that CAGR implies an approximate industry size of **$1.69–1.73 billion** in 2023–2024, though this is modelled/forecast data rather than official statistics. For context, Steel & Tube Holdings (NZX: STU), a major listed steel distributor and fabricator, reported revenue of **$589.1 million** in FY23 and **$479.1 million** in FY24 (year ended 30 June), with volumes declining 21% year-on-year amid the economic slowdown [https://www.nzx.com/announcements/416642, August 2023; https://steelandtube.co.nz/investor/media/2024/steel-tube-fy24-results-year-ended-30-june-2024, 2024].

### (c) NZ Import Tariff Rates for Raw Steel (HS Codes 7207–7216)

The following rates are drawn from the New Zealand Customs Service *Working Tariff Document* (Section XV, Chapter 72) and MBIE trade remedy documentation [https://www.customs.govt.nz/media/45ipk2d3/section-xv.pdf, effective 2025; https://www.mbie.govt.nz/dmsdocument/1546-initiation-review-rebar-thailand-pdf, 2015].

| HS Code | Description | Normal Tariff (MFN) | Preferential Tariff (FTA partners) |
|---------|-------------|---------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **7207** | Semi-finished products of iron/non-alloy steel | **Free** | Free |
| **7208** | Flat-rolled products ≥600mm wide, hot-rolled, not clad/plated/coated | **5%** (selected sub-headings, e.g., 7208.10, 7208.25, 7208.26, 7208.37) | Free (RCEP, China FTA, ASEAN-Australia-NZ, etc.) |
| **7209** | Flat-rolled products ≥600mm wide, cold-rolled, not clad/plated/coated | **Free** | Free |
| **7210** | Flat-rolled products ≥600mm wide, clad/plated/coated | **Free** | Free |
| **7211** | Flat-rolled products <600mm wide, not clad/plated/coated | **Free / 5%** (varies by sub-heading) | Free |
| **7212** | Flat-rolled products <600mm wide, clad/plated/coated | **Free / 5%** (varies by sub-heading) | Free |
| **7213** | Bars and rods, hot-rolled, in irregularly wound coils | **5% or Free** | Free under NZ–Thailand CEP, AANZFTA, etc. |
| **7214** | Other bars and rods of iron/non-alloy steel | **5% or Free** | Free under relevant FTAs |
| **7215** | Other bars and rods of iron/non-alloy steel | **5% or Free** | Free |
| **7216** | Angles, shapes and sections of iron/non-alloy steel | **Free / 5%** (varies by sub-heading) | Free |

**Effective Tariff Impact on Local Fabricator Margins**
Most primary raw steel inputs used by structural fabricators—semi-finished products (7207), cold-rolled flat (7209), and coated products (7210)—enter New Zealand **duty-free** even at MFN rates. Certain hot-rolled flat products (7208) and bar/rod categories (7213–7215) attract a **5% normal tariff**, but under New Zealand’s extensive FTA network (including RCEP, China, ASEAN, Australia, and others), preferential rates for qualifying goods are overwhelmingly **Free** [https://www.mbie.govt.nz/business-and-employment/business/trade-and-tariffs/tariffs-in-new-zealand, May 2022; https://www.customs.govt.nz/media/uwyg054w/fact-sheet-08-preferential-tariff-duty-rates-and-an-explanation-about-the-rules-of-origin-22-sept-2023.pdf, September 2023]. Consequently, the **effective tariff protection for local fabricators on raw material inputs is minimal to nil**. Fabricators face global steel price signals almost directly; the 5% MFN rate on select hot-rolled products offers only marginal buffering and is frequently circumvented via FTA-qualified imports. This low tariff environment benefits fabricators on input costs but also exposes them to fully imported fabricated steel competing on price.

### (d) Three Major Infrastructure Projects (Structural Steel Demand)

**1. City Rail Link (Auckland)**
- **Scope:** 3.45 km twin-tunnel underground railway connecting Waitematā (Britomart) to Maungawhau (Mt Eden), with two new stations (Te Waihorotiu and Karanga-a-Hape).
- **Steel Demand:** Approximately **25,000 tonnes** of locally produced reinforcing steel; significant quantities of structural steel for station boxes, diaphragm walls, and tunnel support. Pacific Steel manufactured 50 mm high-strength reinforcing bars (a NZ first) for the Chief Post Office underpinning.
- **Timeline:** Construction and testing expected complete by end of June 2026; handover to Auckland Transport/KiwiRail for opening in the **second half of 2026**.
- **Sources:** [https://www.cityraillink.co.nz/city-rail-link-project-overview, 2024]; [https://www.nzsteel.co.nz/news-and-media/local-steel-for-aucklands-city-rail-link/, 2024]; [https://nzinfrastructure.nz/aucklands-huge-crl-project-a-win-for-city-and-country/, November 2024].

**2. Te Ara Pekapeka Bridge (Hamilton)**
- **Scope:** 180 m–215 m span across the Waikato River linking Hamilton East to the Peacocke development; part of the Southern Links programme.
- **Steel Demand:** **2,650–3,060 tonnes** of structural steel, including Corten/weathering steel for the central pier, beams, and balustrades. The pier lattice sections each weigh over 200 tonnes.
- **Timeline:** Opened to pedestrians/cyclists **30 August 2024**; fully operational 2024.
- **Sources:** [https://hamilton.govt.nz/strategies-plans-and-projects/projects/peacocke/te-ara-pekapeka-bridge-and-surrounding-transport-network, 2024]; [https://structurae.net/en/structures/te-ara-pekapeka-bridge, 2024]; [https://wbn.co.nz/2024/09/03/te-ara-pekapeka-by-the-numbers/, September 2024].

**3. Waimea Community Dam (Nelson/Tasman)**
- **Scope:** First large-scale dam built in New Zealand in 30 years; 53 m high, 220 m long concrete-faced rock-fill embankment.
- **Steel Demand:** More than **2,000 tonnes** of reinforcing steel; 30,000 cubic metres of concrete.
- **Timeline:** Practical completion **June 2024**; commissioned September 2024; officially opened **February 2025**. Final project cost **$211 million**.
- **Sources:** [https://waimeawater.nz/2025/01/13/final-cost-of-waimea-community-dam-agreed/, January 2025]; [https://www.1news.co.nz/2024/04/15/first-look-nzs-first-large-scale-dam-built-in-the-last-30-years/, April 2024]; [https://www.waterfordpress.co.nz/waimea-water-waimea-dam-development-gamechanger-for-area/, February 2023].

---

### (e) Top 3 Strategic Risks and Opportunities for Fabricators

**1. Exposure to Low-Priced Import Competition (Risk)**
Chinese and other offshore steel imports dominate New Zealand’s steel import market, with China consistently the top source country [https://www.6wresearch.com/industry-report/new-zealand-steel-market, 2024]. Local fabricators face direct price competition from imported fabricated steel and raw materials. In 2023, industry participants expressed concern about increased Chinese steel volumes entering the market amid softening domestic demand [https://businessdesk.co.nz/article/infrastructure/structural-steel-industry-plateaus-with-economic-slowdown, May 2023]. With raw steel tariffs at or near zero under FTAs, fabricators have limited tariff protection against dumped or subsidised product.

**2. Supply Chain Concentration and Input Price Volatility (Risk)**
New Zealand has no domestic iron ore mining and limited primary steelmaking capacity (principally New Zealand Steel at Glenbrook and Pacific Steel’s rebar/wire operations). Most flat-rolled structural sections and plate are imported. This creates concentration risk: global steel mill pricing, freight costs, and exchange rate movements flow directly into fabricator cost bases. Steel & Tube’s FY24 results illustrate the volatility, with a **21% volume decline** and revenue falling **19%** as economic conditions deteriorated [https://steelandtube.co.nz/investor/media/2024/steel-tube-fy24-results-year-ended-30-june-2024, 2024]. Fabricators with limited pricing power face margin compression when input costs rise but construction demand is weak.

**3. Public Infrastructure Pipeline and Seismic Resilience Demand (Opportunity)**
The Crown has allocated approximately **$68 billion** to infrastructure over the next five years, and the National Infrastructure Pipeline projects **$21.4 billion** in spend for 2026 alone [https://steelandtube.co.nz/investor/media/2024/steel-tube-fy24-results-year-ended-30-june-2024, 2024; https://tewaihanga.govt.nz/the-pipeline/pipeline-snapshot, December 2025]. Transport, water, and subdivision projects dominate. Additionally, structural steel’s market share in multi-level construction exceeds **50% nationally** and **80% in Christchurch**, driven by its proven seismic performance [https://scnz.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/03/SCNZ-IU-Mar-23.pdf, March 2023]. As New Zealand continues to strengthen building codes and pursue earthquake-prone building upgrades, demand for locally fabricated, certified structural steel is positioned to grow—provided fabricators can maintain capacity and compete on delivery certainty against imports.

---
